title: HTML Link Semantics, Base URLs, Relations, and Discovery Decisions · Compiler Case: Relation Policy
summary: Every href becomes a page request; base, rel, hreflang, media, downloads, forms, non-navigation links, and nofollow policy are ignored. Predict authorization, URL identity, runtime admission, Promise ownership, resource bounds, accessibility impact, failure, and evidence before reading the governing source. Planned artifact: Add and verify the crawler ts relation policy increment in semantic link-discovery ledger. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, cau…
objectives: Interpret rel tokens, download, target, media, hreflang, canonical, alternate, and nofollow under an explicit discovery policy.
Apply first-valid-base rules against the final response URL and retain base source and rejected alternatives.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Interpret rel tokens, download, target, media, hreflang, canonical, alternate, and nofollow under an explicit discovery policy.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Relation Policy model · Theory Relation Policy, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: rel=nofollow is an authorization rule with one universal meaning.
